text:
I have been relatively quiet this week in the midst of such volatility and with very good reason because I am on a break in China (my very maiden trip here).

What I have seen and experienced so far related to energy:

1️⃣ High speed train reaching a speed of 348 km/h from Beijing to Tianjin, electric powered, no less

2️⃣ Busy roads and highways, quieter than most because of influx of EV vehicles. But it doesn't mean no traditional transport fuelled cars though.

3️⃣ Lots of English words everywhere but not much spoken (good luck with ordering food in a random shop!)

4️⃣ Amazing skyscrapers and construction in many places. Tianjin in particular surprised me with vibrant lights and laser show on the facade of multiple buildings 

5️⃣ A strong sense of culture steeped in thousands of years of history yet striving for innovation and hunger for success

Can't say I have witnessed demand destruction first hand.

Photo taken at Temple of Heaven where I heard some Americans talking to a local about Elon Musk being there recently. I was thinking of Trump more as I walked up those steps!

#oott

MAP OF THE DAY: Iran has published a new chart showing two shipping lanes in Hormuz to the south of the ones that Tehran unveiled weeks ago. The new lanes are to the north of the ones used before the war. The Omani lane remains in operation too.

Brent bears build near-record short position on peace memo, shifting balance of risks

Investors have become very bearish about the outlook for crude oil prices following the conclusion of a memorandum of understanding between the United States and Iran to terminate their war and re-open the Strait of Hormuz.

Hedge funds and other money managers sold the equivalent of 95 million barrels in ICE Brent futures and options over the seven days ending on June 16 (a period that included announcement of the memorandum).

Fund managers sold Brent at the fastest rate for more than a year, and at one of the fastest weekly rates since the coronavirus pandemic ...

This is a short extract from my newsletter emailed to subscribers earlier today. If you would like to read my newsletters in full, three times a week, you can subscribe via my website https://t.co/YjJTctVocX

Drinking from the cup of poison: White House reluctantly catches up with the oil market

Oil traders and hedge funds increasingly anticipated the imminent re-opening of the Strait of Hormuz for more than a month before the United States and Iran concluded a memorandum of understanding ending their war on June 15.

With petroleum inventories at home running down rapidly, top U.S. policymakers faced an increasingly urgent deadline to decide whether to reach an agreement with Iran or attempt to re-open the Strait fully by force. 

Ultimately, the White House seems to have decided the embarrassment of a diplomatic deal achieving less than its stated war aims was a lesser evil than the potential risks of trying to force the Strait open against hostile fire. 

In recent weeks, the threats, blockades, clandestine convoys and attacks on missile and drone facilities near the waterway can all be seen as an effort to escape making this unpleasant choice. 

But after exploring various options, the White House appears to have concluded none of the military ones was acceptable and it would have to settle for a deal achieving much less than it initially hoped when the war started.  

In reaching that decision, the White House was catching up with a conclusion many oil traders and fund managers had reached weeks earlier ...

The cost of shipping #containers continues to surge, with the Drewry Composite Index rising 12% over the past week, extending its seven-week gain to nearly 80%. Freight rates on the key Shanghai–New York and Shanghai–Rotterdam routes have almost doubled during that period, driven by a "perfect storm" of overlapping geopolitical, regulatory, and seasonal supply chain pressures.

In essence, a surge that isn't driven by a boom in global consumer spending, but rather an artificial rush of buying for vessel space to outrun tariffs, regulatory deadlines, and extended transit times at a time of elevated fuel costs. 

The index has risen 79% YTD and 21% over the past twelve months.

Maritime traffic via the Strait of Hormuz will only return to normal levels when mines are removed and traditional routes restored, some of the world's largest shipping organizations said June 18 while calling for more clarity on transit rules and coordination among authorities to allow safe passage. https://t.co/xouak9yKnr
https://t.co/XEMh6w66cm

As Traders Avoid Risk, Stocks Fall & Dollar Rises - #Asia #MarketWrap 
Global equities edged lower in holiday-thinned trading as investors assessed whether a 60-day diplomatic window between the US and Iran could pave the way for a broader nuclear agreement and a more durable peace settlement.

MSCI's global equity index fell 0.2%, trimming weekly gains, while an Asian stock gauge slipped 0.9% after a five-day rally that had carried the benchmark to record highs. S&P 500 futures declined 0.5% following Thursday's 1.1% advance, and the Dollar strengthened against most major peers as investors adopted a more cautious stance heading into the weekend. Market liquidity was reduced by holiday closures across the US, China, Hong Kong and Taiwan.

Brent crude traded below $80/bbl and was on course for a weekly decline of roughly 9%. The sharp drop reflects easing concerns over global energy supplies after the US-Iran interim peace agreement helped restore shipping activity through the Strait of Hormuz, unwinding much of the conflict-related risk premium in oil markets.

Bond yields moved higher across parts of the Asia-Pacific region, with 10-year Japanese and Australian yields advancing. In the US, two-year Treasury yields steadied around 4.18% after surging 13 bps in the previous session to their highest level in more than a year as traders increased bets on future Fed tightening following the central bank's hawkish hold.

Gold was on track for a third consecutive weekly loss as expectations for higher interest rates outweighed optimism surrounding the peace agreement. Adding to the pressure, Goldman Sachs cut its year-end gold price forecast by $500/oz, reflecting a less supportive outlook for the precious metal amid rising real yields and a stronger Dollar.

🚨 Oil Producers Say “Never Again” to Strait of Hormuz — Structural Shift After Conflict

Even after the US-Iran ceasefire reopens the Strait, oil-producing countries are sticking with alternative routes.

Goldman Sachs forecast:
• Pre-war 100% → Post-war ~70% traffic levels
• Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iraq ramping up bypass pipelines & new ports
• UAE officially declares “Zero dependence on Hormuz”

After 107 days of blockade, Gulf nations built permanent alternatives they’re not abandoning.

This could reshape global oil supply chains and energy security long-term.

For import-dependent countries like Korea, this is a wake-up call on energy diversification.

What do you think — how will this impact global oil prices and energy markets going forward? 👇

**Iran oil, AI tax, institutional #BTC buys—markets sending mixed signals? 🔀**

Goldman slashes gold forecast to $4,900. Meanwhile, Franklin Templeton just filed for a Bitcoin DRIP ETF. Institutions keep stacking while analysts cut precious metals.

Bernie Sanders wants a 50% one-time tax on AI company stocks to seed a $7T public fund. Think about regulatory risk hitting tech valuations.

Iran exporting ~18M barrels in 5 days. New Strait of Hormuz rules. Crude buyers in Asia getting pressure.

My take: Don't chase the narrative. The same banks pushing crypto adoption are also killing your gold exposure. Geopolitical risk is real, but so is policy overreach. Diversify, stay liquid, and never confuse institutional PR for alpha.

text:
Markets Pause After Strong Week as Focus Shifts to Lasting Iran Deal – #US #MarketWrap 
Global equities ended the week on a more cautious footing as initial relief over the interim US-Iran peace agreement gave way to questions about whether a durable long-term settlement can be achieved.

With US cash markets closed for the Juneteenth holiday, S&P 500 futures moved lower after the benchmark recorded its strongest weekly performance since late May. European stocks edged down 0.2%, while Asian equities slipped 0.3% from record highs. Markets in China, Hong Kong, and Taiwan were closed.

Attention turned to the diplomatic process after talks on a permanent agreement scheduled for Friday in Switzerland were delayed. The postponement followed overnight clashes between Israel and Hezbollah in Lebanon, highlighting the broader regional tensions that continue to complicate negotiations.

Later developments offered some encouragement, with Israel and Hezbollah agreeing to a ceasefire. Iran has made stability in Lebanon a key condition of its preliminary agreement with the United States, underscoring how interconnected the various regional conflicts have become.

Oil prices rebounded, with Brent crude rising 0.9% to above $80 a barrel, though it remained well below levels seen earlier in the conflict. Activity through the Strait of Hormuz appeared to slow again after a brief recovery in shipping flows, while Tehran said vessels transiting the waterway would require its permission, a reminder that risks to global energy supplies have not fully disappeared.

As Iran Nuclear Talks Face an Early Obstacle, Stocks Slide – #Europe #MarketWrap 
Global equities ended the week on a cautious note as optimism surrounding the US-Iran interim peace agreement gave way to concerns over the difficulty of securing a lasting settlement in the region.

With US cash markets closed for the Juneteenth holiday, S&P 500 futures fell 0.3% after the benchmark recorded its strongest weekly performance since late May. European equities were little changed, while an index of Asian stocks slipped 0.4% from record highs. Trading volumes were subdued with markets in China, Hong Kong and Taiwan also closed.

Attention shifted back to geopolitical risks after planned talks on a permanent agreement between Washington and Tehran were postponed. The delay came after overnight clashes between Israel and Iran-backed Hezbollah forces in Lebanon. Reports indicated the renewed tensions contributed to the postponement, with Iran making a ceasefire in Lebanon a condition of its preliminary agreement with the US.

Brent crude fluctuated around $80/bbl as traders assessed the outlook for energy supplies. Shipping activity through the Strait of Hormuz appeared to slow after a brief surge following the announcement that the US and Iran would begin lifting restrictions on traffic through the key waterway.

Bond markets also drew attention. UK gilts led declines across European sovereign debt after Andy Burnham secured a seat in Parliament, potentially positioning him as a future challenger to Starmer. Investors are assessing whether a Burnham-led government could pursue looser fiscal policies, a prospect that pressured UK bonds and pushed yields higher.

Overall, markets remain caught between easing concerns over Middle East energy disruptions and uncertainty over whether recent diplomatic progress can evolve into a broader and more durable regional settlement.

Trump is the gravest threat to our "national security."

He has single-handedly weakened our alliances and strengthened the strategic, geopolitical and economic positions of our global adversaries.

Under Trump, US credibility is collapsing, Iran and Russia are being admitted to the international club, China is filling the void left by USAID's elimination and is capitalizing on the waning soft power of the US around the globe.

NATO is fracturing, and we've tarnished whatever democratic integrity we had left on the world stage.

Immediately after entering office, Trump abandoned his isolationist ethos and started talking about conquering Greenland, annexing Canada and seizing international trade routes.

This alone has been problematic for our alliances.

Meanwhile, the greatest beneficiaries of the war in Iran have been Russia, Iran, and China.

The US has conceded bargaining power to Iran on all matters. The Iranian regime now has the leverage to extract massive concessions from the US, especially when it comes to their control over the Strait, their nuclear and ballistic missiles programs, their support for terrorist proxies, and the very sovereignty of this more deeply entrenched regime.

The US is also setting up opportunities for Iran to cash in on massive windfalls.

The US is signaling that it's going to waive Iranian banking and oil sanctions; allowing them to sell oil globally at free market rates. The revenue Iran stands to gain from this could be massive.

Moreover, JD Vance claims that the IRGC has seen the error of its ways, and both him and Jared Kushner have indicated that alongside unfrozen assets, the US will establish a $300 BILLION "reconstruction" fund for Iran that will include private investments from around the world, which will no doubt come with its own kickbacks for Trump's allies.

Kushner himself said it would help "corral international investment in iran"

Donald Trump went from calling Obama's JCPOA one of the worst deals he's ever seen, to walking head first into an agreement that's materially worse in every way, and not just on matters of nuclear proliferation, but in ways that normalize relationships with Iran and unlock massive financial benefits for the Iranian regime.

And to make matters worse, this war has further radicalized our enemies against us, enemies who are now being welcomed into the international arena.

Before Trump's war, Russia's economy was facing massive hurdles due to economic sanctions and the cost of the war itself. 

Russia's federal budget relies on oil prices, and because the war caused Brent crude prices to surge, the US incidentally provided the Kremlin with the capital it needs to sustain its war efforts in Ukraine. 

Both Russia AND China have also been supplying Iran with high tech strategic resources, missile guidance systems, satellite imagery and intelligence on American assets.

This war has also created diplomatic rifts between the US and its allies.

Trump has been alienating both our European and Persian Gulf allies while threatening to upend our alliances altogether.

And now our once friendly democratic partners are trying to establish a new world order that excludes the US. Can you blame them?

Even before the war, Trump was souring our trade partners against us due to his reckless and unilateral approach to tariffs and global trade policy.

In some cases, these economic allies have turned to China for trade, contributing to their trade surplus and their ongoing advancements in manufacturing, AI and automation.

China could also benefit immensely from an IRGC controlled toll system.

Also, because Trump has devoted the US's attention and resources to another pointless war in the middle east, there's less strategic focus on Asia, which China is no doubt aware of and is planning accordingly.

Worse yet, thanks to Trump, the Russia-China-Iran axis has hardened. Where it was once a cautious relationship, it is now a more coordinated one.

WTI Crude $Oil (Futures) - 20 JUNE 2026 - Update

This is why alternative scenarios always matter. 

Elliott Wave deals in probabilities, not certainties. The preferred count is your best guess, not a guarantee. Markets have a way of humbling anyone who forgets that. https://t.co/A2GpO0JSuy

Per Grok regarding agreements made with Iran (MOU):

 The most recent agreement is the Islamabad Memorandum of Understanding (MOU), signed electronically around June 17, 2026, between the US and Iran. It ended recent hostilities, including a conflict that disrupted the Strait of Hormuz. This 14-point framework includes: - Immediate ceasefire — Permanent end to military operations on all fronts, including Lebanon, with no threats or use of force, and respect for sovereignty. - Strait of Hormuz — Iran ensures safe, toll-free passage for commercial vessels (initially 60 days), with de-mining within 30 days. The US lifts its naval blockade within 30 days. - Nuclear commitments — Iran reaffirms it will not develop or procure nuclear weapons. They’ll negotiate the disposition of its enriched uranium stockpile (minimum: down-blending on-site under IAEA supervision) and future enrichment limits in a final deal. Both sides maintain the nuclear status quo for now. - Sanctions and economy — The US issues immediate oil export waivers and makes frozen Iranian assets usable. Full sanctions relief (including primary/secondary US sanctions and UN resolutions) is to be scheduled in the final deal. A plan with at least $300 billion in reconstruction aid from the US and regional partners is outlined. - Timeline — 60 days (extendable) for a final, comprehensive deal, to be endorsed by a UN Security Council resolution. An executive mechanism will monitor compliance. It’s a short framework agreement, not a detailed treaty like the old JCPOA — many specifics, especially on nuclear limits, are deferred. Critics on both sides have raised concerns about sanctions relief without stronger upfront nuclear curbs. The 2015 Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA) , or Iran nuclear deal, was a much more detailed multilateral agreement between Iran, the P5+1 (US, UK, France, China, Russia, Germany), and the EU. Iran accepted major limits on its nuclear program (centrifuge numbers, enrichment levels capped at 3.67%, stockpile caps, facility conversions) in exchange for sanctions relief. The US withdrew in 2018, Iran later exceeded the limits, and the deal effectively collapsed. The new MOU appears to be an attempt at a fresh start after conflict, but it’s narrower so far and focuses more on ending the immediate war. These are the main publicly known agreements. The next 60 days of talks will determine if a more permanent nuclear deal emerges. Things are still fluid — details could shift.

Why do you post total bs?  Grok says:   The claim is false: No credible reports confirm Iran reclosing the Strait of Hormuz or Trump’s interim peace deal collapsing; a US-Iran MOU signed June 17 explicitly requires Iran to allow safe, toll-free passage through the strait for 60 days as part of ending the war and lifting the US naval blockade.
•  Deal status: The agreement is recent and interim (with 60 days for fuller terms including nuclear issues); shipping remains limited due to lingering mines, insurance, and logistics from prior closure, but traffic has begun and oil prices have eased, with no collapse reported.
•  Post context: Replies widely label it clickbait or misinformation, aligning with the author’s pattern of sensational headlines; the Hormuz situation stems from earlier 2026 conflict escalations, now addressed via the MOU mediated in part by Pakistan.

The Ripple Effect: How the Iran Ceasefire Reshapes the Russia-Ukraine War
The signing of the Islamabad Memorandum and the subsequent ceasefire between the US and Iran may appear to be a localized de-escalation in the Middle East. In reality, it functions as a precise geopolitical "resource drain" that fundamentally disrupts Russia's strategic balance in the Russia-Ukraine war. The profound impacts unfold across four critical dimensions:
 1. Russia's Mounting Fiscal Pressures
In 2026, the structural strain on Russia’s wartime economy has become increasingly acute. According to recent economic data, the liquid assets of Russia’s National Wealth Fund have plummeted to approximately 1.8% of GDP, down from 6.5% at the war's onset, pushing the federal budget deficit near its breaking point. Complicated by Ukraine’s recent high-frequency drone strikes on the Moscow Oil Refinery and southern fuel depots, the Kremlin faces a double financial black hole: sustaining front-line military expenditures while absorbing soaring domestic air defense and repair costs. The Middle East ceasefire strips away the geopolitical buffer that delayed Russia’s economic reckoning, pushing its budget system toward a critical tipping point.
 2. The End of High Oil Price Dividends (Including India)
The previous maritime blockade in the Strait of Hormuz drove global energy premiums through the roof, resulting in a financial windfall for Russia. To stabilize Asian energy markets during the crisis, Western regulators temporarily tolerated countries like India aggressively buying Russian crude via "shadow fleets." India capitalized on cheap energy and lucrative re-export margins, while Moscow secured a massive lifeline of dark-pool foreign exchange. With the Strait fully reopened, global oil risk premiums have evaporated. Faced with slumping crude prices and escalating Western secondary sanction risks, core buyers like India are bound to curb their panic-buying, abruptly severing Russia’s most reliable economic pipeline.
 3. Geopolitical Pivot and Resource Realignment
For months, Western funds, intelligence apparatuses, and critical defense manufacturing—especially Patriot air defense interceptor stockpiles—were heavily drained by the escalation in the Middle East. This strategic distraction left Ukraine facing severe logistical shortfalls and ammo starvation on the front lines. Now that the Middle East has cooled down, Western allies have successfully freed up their operational bandwidth, realigning their diplomatic weight and military production back toward Eastern Europe. The G7 summit quickly crystallized a renewed consensus for maximum pressure on Moscow, unlocking a fresh $4 billion aid package for President Zelenskyy. This resource pivot explains Ukraine's sudden tactical confidence in launching the recent "logistics lockdown" against Crimea's supply corridors.
 4. Destruction and Retraction of Iranian Production Capabilities
During the heavy exchanges of fire prior to the ceasefire, Iran’s domestic drone assembly lines and missile production facilities sustained severe physical damage, deeply crippling its manufacturing inputs. As Tehran enters a formal 60-day peace negotiation window, its primary objective is ensuring the unfreezing of tens of billions of dollars in overseas assets and achieving relief from suffocating economic sanctions. Consequently, Tehran is highly incentivized to scale back its defense cooperation with Moscow, enforcing a strict retraction on the overt supply of Shahed-series loitering munitions and ballistic technologies. In effect, Russia loses its most strategic partner in absorbing and deflecting Western military pressure.

Iran keeps the rope.

The Strait of Hormuz is being treated like a hostage, released only long enough to yank it back when things go south.

America cannot keep letting Iran use an international waterway as a chokehold on energy, oil prices, and entire economies.

Watch the clip. Share this. Full Video on rumble.

Stop paying ransom.

#Iran #StraitOfHormuz #OilPrices #EnergySecurity #MiddleEast #AmericaFirst #NationalSecurity

text:
𝐓𝐑𝐔𝐌𝐏 𝐀𝐓 𝐆𝟕 𝐁𝐑𝐀𝐍𝐃𝐒 𝐇𝐀𝐖𝐊 𝐂𝐑𝐈𝐓𝐈𝐂𝐒 𝐒𝐓𝐔𝐏𝐈𝐃 — 𝐄𝐕𝐄𝐑𝐘 𝐖𝐎𝐑𝐋𝐃 𝐋𝐄𝐀𝐃𝐄𝐑 𝐂𝐇𝐄𝐄𝐑𝐄𝐃, 𝐓𝐖𝐎 𝐁𝐑𝐔𝐓𝐀𝐋 𝐍𝐈𝐆𝐇𝐓𝐒, 𝐈𝐑𝐀𝐍 𝐍𝐀𝐕𝐘 𝐒𝐔𝐍𝐊

The question at the G7 press conference seemed designed to put Trump on defense: were any world leaders there pressuring him to keep bombing Iran? Trump’s answer erased the premise.

“𝘛𝘩𝘦𝘳𝘦’𝘴 𝘯𝘰𝘵 𝘰𝘯𝘦 𝘯𝘢𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯 𝘵𝘩𝘢𝘵 𝘤𝘢𝘮𝘦 𝘵𝘰 𝘶𝘴 𝘢𝘯𝘥 𝘴𝘢𝘪𝘥, 𝘱𝘭𝘦𝘢𝘴𝘦, 𝘴𝘪𝘳, 𝘬𝘦𝘦𝘱 𝘥𝘳𝘰𝘱𝘱𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘣𝘰𝘮𝘣𝘴 𝘰𝘯 𝘵𝘩𝘦𝘮. 𝘗𝘭𝘦𝘢𝘴𝘦 𝘬𝘦𝘦𝘱 𝘥𝘳𝘰𝘱𝘱𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘣𝘰𝘮𝘣𝘴. 𝘛𝘩𝘦 𝘴𝘵𝘶𝘱𝘪𝘥 𝘱𝘦𝘰𝘱𝘭𝘦 𝘴𝘢𝘺 𝘵𝘩𝘢𝘵.”

Not one. France, Germany, Italy, Japan, Canada, the UK — 𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲 𝐆𝟕 𝐠𝐨𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐰𝐚𝐬 𝐫𝐞𝐥𝐢𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐝 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐨𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐞𝐧𝐝𝐞𝐝. Not reluctantly. Relieved. Because they all had something at stake in a nuclear-armed Iran, and they all had economies dependent on the Strait of Hormuz staying open.

Trump then explained the military calculus the bomb-more crowd refuses to engage with. The last two nights of Operation Epic Fury alone cost 𝐦𝐨𝐫𝐞 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐧 $𝟐𝟎𝟎 𝐦𝐢𝐥𝐥𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐢𝐧 𝐦𝐮𝐧𝐢𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬. Iran was informed a third night was already scheduled.

“𝘛𝘩𝘰𝘴𝘦 𝘭𝘢𝘴𝘵 𝘵𝘸𝘰 𝘥𝘢𝘺𝘴 𝘸𝘦𝘳𝘦 𝘣𝘳𝘶𝘵𝘢𝘭. $200 𝘮𝘪𝘭𝘭𝘪𝘰𝘯 𝘸𝘰𝘳𝘵𝘩 𝘰𝘧 𝘣𝘰𝘮𝘣𝘴. 𝘈𝘯𝘥 𝘣𝘺 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘸𝘢𝘺, 𝘵𝘩𝘦𝘺 𝘬𝘯𝘦𝘸 𝘐 𝘸𝘢𝘴 𝘤𝘰𝘮𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘧𝘰𝘳 𝘢 𝘵𝘩𝘪𝘳𝘥 𝘯𝘪𝘨𝘩𝘵. 𝘞𝘦 𝘪𝘯𝘧𝘰𝘳𝘮𝘦𝘥 𝘵𝘩𝘦𝘮 𝘸𝘦’𝘳𝘦 𝘤𝘰𝘮𝘪𝘯𝘨 𝘧𝘰𝘳 𝘢 𝘵𝘩𝘪𝘳𝘥 𝘯𝘪𝘨𝘩𝘵.”

By that point Iran’s Navy had been destroyed. Their Air Force had been devastated. Their nuclear sites were collapsed under B-2 strikes. They were out of military assets with no path back.

The critics who call this too soft refuse to engage with the economics. Global oil shipping through the Strait of Hormuz represents $𝟓𝟎𝟎 𝐭𝐨 $𝟕𝟎𝟎 𝐦𝐢𝐥𝐥𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐚 𝐝𝐚𝐲 𝐢𝐧 𝐠𝐥𝐨𝐛𝐚𝐥 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐦𝐞𝐫𝐜𝐞. Strategic oil reserves globally had roughly four weeks of runway. Indefinite bombing was not a tougher strategy — it was an energy crisis in slow motion.

The markets gave their verdict. The Dow surged 607 points the day the Iran deal was announced. Oil dropped 5 percent. The S&P gained 1.3 percent. Every major index priced in exactly what Trump delivered: an end to the conflict without an economic collapse.

Analysis of the US Aftermarket and Market Performance for the Week of ~June 15–19, 2026, in the Context of the “Periodic Table of Socio-Economic Formations.”

The table divides history into formations based on key parameters: productive force/power, source of energy, subject of power, resource, and level of freedom.Capitalism is described as:  Productive force — Democracy  
Source of energy — Thermal combustion (oil, gas, coal)  
Subject of power — Capitalist  
Resource — Capital  
Level of freedom — Professional union rights

The last (yellow) row is “What is next?” across all columns. Last week’s market events fit this framework perfectly and give clear hints about the content of those empty cells.What Happened in the US Market Last Week (June 15–19, 2026)The week was volatile but ended with a positive bias, especially in the technology sector.Key drivers:  Geopolitics and energy: Tension around Iran and the Strait of Hormuz (critical for oil supplies) created pressure on oil prices and the broader market at the beginning of the week. Hopes for de-escalation and diplomatic efforts in the Middle East triggered a recovery.  
Historic SpaceX IPO (the largest in US history): Priced on June 12 at $135 per share, raising approximately $75 billion+. Shares jumped more than 19–20% on debut and continued rising strongly on Monday, June 15 (+20% intraday). Market capitalization quickly exceeded $2 trillion.

Index Performance (approximate weekly results):  Nasdaq led thanks to tech and SpaceX.  
S&P 500 and Dow showed gains on several days (strong rebound on Monday: +1.7% and +0.9% respectively), despite some pullbacks related to Fed expectations.  
By the end of the week (June 19 — Juneteenth, markets closed or shortened), the S&P 500 was around 7480–7500 and the Dow near 51,000+.

After-Hours Trading:
Particularly active around the SpaceX IPO — shares continued to trade positively in after-hours on investor enthusiasm. Other days showed typical after-hours movement: individual tech names (semiconductors, AI-related) moving on news, without systemic sell-offs. Overall after-hours sentiment reflected optimism about new technologies rather than fear of old risks (oil/geopolitics).How This Fits the TableCapitalism (current row) clearly showed its vulnerabilities:  Source of energy “thermal combustion” — directly tied to geopolitics (Iran, Strait of Hormuz). Any escalation hurts the market; de-escalation immediately triggers a rebound. Classic dependency of the current formation.  
Resource “capital” and subject “capitalist” — still functioning, but a powerful new player is emerging.

The SpaceX IPO and its subsequent surge signal a transition:  New subject of power — not just a traditional “capitalist,” but a techno-visionary / space-technology corporation (SpaceX + Starlink + AI potential). This is no longer a classic industrial capitalist, but an actor that simultaneously builds infrastructure (connectivity, space) and shapes future markets.  
New resource — outer space, global network data (Starlink), computing power, and AI. Capital hasn’t disappeared, but information + space + technology are moving to the forefront.  
New source of energy/production — moving beyond pure “thermal combustion.” Space technologies open pathways to space-based solar power, new materials, and potentially nuclear/fusion solutions in a fully electrified and automated economy.  
Productive force — no longer just democracy, but technological progress, automation, and AI as the main engine of growth.  
Level of freedom — union rights (current cell) may evolve toward technologically enabled abundance (cheap global connectivity, automation of routine labor, potential post-labor society).

🇨🇭 🇺🇲 🇮🇷 Switzerland said on Friday that planned talks between the United States and Iran to end the Middle East conflict had been postponed, shortly after US Vice-President JD Vance scrapped his scheduled trip to Geneva, deepening uncertainties over whether a durable truce can be reached.

⭕️ The signing of an interim 14-point accord this past week was designed to end the conflict, reopen the Strait of Hormuz and launch a 60-day window covering core issues including Iran's nuclear program. Nevertheless, the ceasefire failed to halt hostilities in Lebanon, as the Israeli military launched fresh airstrikes across southern Lebanon on Friday.

🛢🛢 Global oil prices tumbled on Friday to the lowest level since March 2. A total of 25 commercial vessels crossed the newly reopened Strait of Hormuz on Thursday, the highest number since mid-April, according to data from maritime tracking firm AXSMarine published on Friday. Lloyd's List earlier estimated that around 550 merchant vessels were waiting to leave the Persian Gulf.

The physical market gives a different read. US crude inventories are at their lowest since 1985, yet WTI calendar spreads haven't reflected that inventory tightness the way you'd expect. OPEC+ is simultaneously rolling back quota cuts while holding over 5 million barrels per day of spare capacity — so the price signal is navigating both a geopolitical risk reprice and a structural supply overhang. The divergence between physical tightness and financial price usually resolves in favor of the inventory signal, but the spare capacity buffer is what makes this cycle unusual.

This is the official story from the US Government, the EIA:

US Commercial Crude Oil Inventories were drawn down by -8.26m barrels for the week ending June 12th.  This brought US Commercial Crude inventories to 418.2m barrels.

The average draw over the last four weeks is -6.71m.

If we assume a weekly draw of 6.71m barrels for the week ending today, June 19th, that would bring commercial inventories down to 411.5m barrels.

The operational floor is said to be around 350m barrels.  Oil in pipelines can’t be drained, the pipelines require hydraulic pressure.  Sludge in the bottom of tanks, etc.

This leaves US Commercial Crude Inventories at just 61.5m barrels.  With an average draw of -6.7m barrels per week, the US has just nine weeks left.

Iran just closed the Strait again.  JD Vance has cancelled his trip to Switzerland to sign a peace agreement with Iran.  The clock is ticking.  Brent short interest is at a 5-year high.

At what point do the markets panic?  8 weeks?  7 weeks?  6 weeks?  It could be any day now.  Cushing was at the operational minimum inventory level of 20m barrels as of June 12th.

text:
US crude inventories are at their lowest since 1985, yet WTI timespreads are not signalling the tightness you might expect.   

In the week ending 12 June, US crude stocks drew by 8.3 mb, taking outright levels to their lowest since 1985. Cushing fell to levels near operational minimums, and PADD 2 stocks hit their lowest since 2014. But the latest 40 mb SPR exchange, higher Permian crude inflows to Cushing and the announcement of a US–Iran MoU are all working to ease the near-term supply picture.   

Across products, the MoU is already shifting trade economics: jet export demand from the Gulf Coast is expected to slow, while Asian LPG buyers have adopted a wait-and-see stance ahead of potential freight rate easing through Hormuz. Our latest US Oil Weekly breaks down the draw across all the barrels.   

Read more: https://t.co/byGRebrgDK

**Exclusive** details I gleaned today about the seized sanctioned unflagged Russian oil tanker. Note there are 24 Indian and Georgian crew still detained on this vessel.
-The seized Russian vessel is detained outside port limits at an anchorage outside Weymouth, Dorset. It is being monitored 24/7 by the appropriate authorities while it is under detention.
-The UK Department for Transport is receiving regular updates on the crew’s welfare and the vessel’s maintenance. 
-The ship owner remains legally responsible for the crew and vessel, including providing required provisions and arranging any onward travel should the crew wish to disembark.
-Any crew disembarking an interdicted vessel, subject to them meeting the relevant requirements, would be permitted to enter the UK solely to return to their country of residence.

**Around 26 ships transited the Strait of Hormuz in the most recent reported 24-hour period (as of June 18–19, 2026).**<grok:render card_id=“c87563” card_type=“citation_card” type=“render_inline_citation”><argument name="citation_id">2</argument></grok:render><grok:render card_id=“e2ff7a” card_type=“citation_card” type=“render_inline_citation”><argument name="citation_id">8</argument></grok:render>

This figure comes from multiple maritime intelligence sources (e.g., https://t.co/ePGaytunEj and Windward) tracking AIS data and other observations during the ongoing partial recovery after a US-Iran framework agreement/ceasefire. It breaks down roughly as 7 inbound and 19 outbound in one detailed report, with some vessels (including Iranian-flagged or sanctioned ones) using northern corridors or running dark (AIS off).<grok:render card_id=“492fe8” card_type=“citation_card” type=“render_inline_citation”><argument name="citation_id">37</argument></grok:render>

### Context on Current Traffic

- **Normal pre-crisis levels**: ~60 ships per day on average (with significant oil/tanker traffic making up a large share).<grok:render card_id=“0ce71a” card_type=“citation_card” type=“render_inline_citation”><argument name="citation_id">2</argument></grok:render>
- **Recent trends**: Traffic has been severely disrupted since late February 2026 due to conflict, Iranian actions, and a US blockade. Daily transits were often in the single digits to low teens earlier in June, with a surge to ~25–26 after the recent deal.<grok:render card_id=“ed0a22” card_type=“citation_card” type=“render_inline_citation”><argument name="citation_id">0</argument></grok:render><grok:render card_id=“766873” card_type=“citation_card” type=“render_inline_citation”><argument name="citation_id">5</argument></grok:render>
- **Other reports**: Some Iranian sources (e.g., IRGC) have claimed higher numbers like 35 in a 24-hour window, while trackers like Shipfinder showed only ~4 on certain recent days—highlighting discrepancies due to AIS gaps, dark vessels, or differing definitions of “transit.”<grok:render card_id=“204868” card_type=“citation_card” type=“render_inline_citation”><argument name="citation_id">5</argument></grok:render>

Live dashboards (e.g., MarineTraffic, https://t.co/KoBRxgLyg7, or specialized monitors) show 4–17 vessels actively in or near the strait at any given moment, with volatility as reopening proceeds amid lingering risks (mines, insurance, stranded vessels).<grok:render card_id=“236455” card_type=“citation_card” type=“render_inline_citation”><argument name="citation_id">7</argument></grok:render>

Exact counts can vary by source and exact timeframe because of incomplete AIS data and geopolitical sensitivities. For the absolute latest, check real-time trackers like MarineTraffic or https://t.co/ePGaytunEj. Traffic is recovering but remains well below normal.

# The $50 Billion Breakup: UAE Just Quit the World's Oldest Oil Cartel

The best student in class just tore up the homework in front of everyone.

After 59 years of playing by OPEC's rules, the United Arab Emirates walked out. Not a "we need to talk" moment. A full exit from both OPEC and OPEC+. The kind of breakup where you don't ask for your stuff back, you just ghost.

Here's the tension: UAE was never the troublemaker. That was Venezuela, or Libya, or whoever was cheating on quotas that month. UAE was the reliable one. Showed up to meetings. Cut production when asked. Smiled for the cameras next to the Saudis.

But reliable doesn't mean stupid.

**The membership card was bleeding money.**

UAE can pump around 4.2 million barrels per day at some of the lowest costs in the world — under $10 per barrel. OPEC+ quotas? They've been sitting around 3 million barrels daily, sometimes lower during aggressive cuts. That's over 1 million barrels per day left in the ground. At $70 oil, that's $25 billion annually in revenue they're watching evaporate while playing team player.

Meanwhile, U.S. shale producers are pumping 13+ million barrels per day with zero quota constraints. Every time OPEC cuts, American frackers fill the gap and bank the margin.

The math stopped mathing.

**The exit was premeditated, not emotional.**

UAE didn't wake up angry one morning. They've been building the divorce file for years. Sovereign wealth funds now manage over $1.7 trillion across tech, infrastructure, renewables. ADNOC, their national oil company, has expansion projects ready to roll that could push capacity past 5 million barrels per day by 2027.

They also hedged geopolitically. Long-term LNG deals with Asia. Refining partnerships that lock in crude demand regardless of OPEC drama. Even their Abraham Accords positioning created commercial channels that don't run through Riyadh.

This wasn't a rage-quit. It was a calculated unbundling.

**Now the dominoes get interesting.**

UAE wasn't the only one gritting teeth during quota meetings. Kazakhstan has been vocally frustrated. Iraq routinely overproduces and pays lip service to cuts. Even smaller players like Republic of Congo have complained about being capped while sitting on expansion potential.

If UAE walks without consequence — or worse, if they thrive — the message is clear: the cartel can't punish defection anymore.

Saudi Arabia and Russia can keep their "OPEC+ alliance," but it's starting to look less like a coalition and more like a duet where everyone else is backup singers with muted mics.

Riyadh's leverage was always "we'll flood the market if you don't comply." But UAE calling that bluff exposes the flaw: Saudi Arabia needs $80+ oil to fund Vision 2030 and keep the budget balanced. They can't afford a price war any more than the members they're trying to discipline.

**What this means for oil markets: chaos dressed as freedom.**

For decades, OPEC was the price floor. A cartel with all the bugs of collusion but one killer feature—predictability. Traders knew that below a certain price, the Saudis would coordinate cuts. That backstop is now cracking.

Without it, oil becomes more like a normal commodity. More volatile. More responsive to actual supply and demand instead of backroom quotas. 

Consumers in importing nations (China, India, Europe) might quietly cheer. More supply competition theoretically means lower structural prices. But it also means whiplash. No stabilizer when geopolitics or recessions hit.

High-cost producers are sweating. If UAE floods the market and others follow, the breakeven squeeze hits Canada's oil sands, Brazil's deepwater, and even U.S. shale's marginal wells.

The biggest tell? UAE didn't leave because oil is dying. They left because they think they can make *more* money outside the club than in it.

**When the honor roll student starts cutting class, the principal has already lost control.**
